title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
short_title: Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get extra time to file their taxes when natural disasters happen. Right now, only the federal government can give people more time when disasters are declared, but this law would let state governors ask for tax deadline extensions too when they declare disasters in their states. The bill covers disasters like hurricanes, floods, earthquakes, and fires that cause serious damage. It also gives people more time by increasing the automatic extension from 60 days to 120 days for people who are hurt by disasters or have their homes or businesses damaged.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Filing Relief for Natural Disasters Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Modification of Rules for Postponing Certain Deadlines by Reason of Disaster
sections.text: This section changes the tax rules about delaying tax deadlines when disasters happen. First, it gives new power to postpone federal tax deadlines when states declare disasters. The Treasury Secretary can work with the Federal Emergency Management Agency to apply disaster relief rules when a state governor or the mayor of Washington DC asks in writing. A qualified state declared disaster means any natural disaster like hurricanes, tornadoes, storms, floods, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, mudslides, snowstorms, or droughts that happen in any part of a state. It also includes any fire, flood, or explosion regardless of what caused it. The governor or mayor must decide that the damage is severe enough to need these special tax rules. The word state includes Washington DC, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Second, this section extends the time limit for mandatory extensions from sixty days to one hundred twenty days. Third, these changes will apply to disaster declarations made after this law is signed.
